author: @justtinkering6713
text: That fast oscillation is not useless. You just made a dc boost circuit that's electromechanical. Put in a diode and a filter capacitor run the relay from 5 volts dc and you should get a much higher voltage output. Maybe 80 volts.

author: @1magnit
text: You've just re invented the electro mechanical telephone exchange. The timing pulses originate at the clock, as in a real clock that tells the time. They output a 1 second pulse as well as a 30 sec pulse which effectively controls everything which requires some sort of timing.

author: @jeremyboyce7921

Thanks Robert that’s awesome.  I’ve been thinking of a simple solar tracker, so you have given me some ideas.  I’ve been thinking of slim panels that can be rotated as opposed to a large billboard.

I don’t think slim line panels are on comparison to larger ones, but it’s worth thinking about.  Slim line panels need to come down and mounting for severe weather needs to be solved.

author: @hanslepoeter5167
text:
Fun. I did that as a kid, Every time the contacts interrupt a high voltage spike is induced in the coil ( See the large spark between contacts ? That's why ). Using a diode and capacitor you can collect that charge and you have a high voltage generator. Nothing dangerous if you use below 100 nF.

I do the same thing in maintenance as well if I happen to come across a relay with high resistance contacts. Running it like that burns contacts clean in seconds. 

Slowing it down like that is nice but the thing is there is no way to get let's say a 50% duty cycle. You will not be able to drive your steppermotor with such a circuit ( maybe 2 relays could work but I'm not sure ). Of course a microcontroller is the best way to use as virtually all will support an exact timing based on crystal accuracy which is just a few ppm.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: I am afraid there is mate - I might do a video on it but basically with 5 SPDT relays wired up you can change the duty cycle

author: @paulwright5440
text: Could you not just use a transistor

replies.author: @jamesyoungquist6923
replies.text: Obviously, not the point of the video

replies.author: @paulwright5440
replies.text: @jamesyoungquist6923   but it could switch on and off a thousand times

replies.author: @jamesyoungquist6923
replies.text: @paulwright5440  yes that's true and he said that, and showed several schematics using transistors as alternatives. This project was specifically to see if it was possible with a mechanical relay

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: no mate - not by itself - the transistor is just a switch in this and you need something to turn it off and on at a set frequency - an oscillator - here the relay is the oscillator

replies.author: @jamesyoungquist6923
replies.text: @ThinkingandTinkering  a ring oscillator from an odd number of purely transistor based Not gates does work, based on the transistor rise and fall time. But you'd still want to tune the frequency with a potentiometer

author: @bartronicsecurity
text: That was how I basically test the quality of the relays I use in my products. I could create an electronic switching circuit to operate the relay coil but I found that making the relay do the work along with a big cap and a 12 ohm current limiting resistor in series with the cap did the work for me. I connected the n/c contact to a counter which when it stopped counting meant that the contacts had burned and the relay was now useless. Best relay ever tested was Takamasowa 822 thousand at 1 amp switching on the contacts. Next was Fujitsu at 676 thousand also at 1 amp on the contacts. Many cheap and nasties don't even make it to 100 thousand.

author: @ClivePenfold
text: I built a relay based oscillator 10 years ago for an interactive model, and it is still working. The large capacitor is in series with the coil which pulls in whilst it is charging. When the relay drops out due to the cap being nearly charged, the contacts discharge the cap via a a resistor of a couple of ohms.

author: @bsadewitz
text: When you first started explaining this, I recalled that the tone generators for electromechanical telephone switches were typically motors with several different cams which yielded the desired frequencies.  And so I wondered, "Why did they choose that instead of a circuit like this?" But then you answered the question: durability.  Seems obvious in hindsight, heh.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: everything is 20:20 in hindsight mate lol

replies.author: @velcroman11
replies.text: The reason for the use on the “ringing machine” was durability.   These 50V machines would run for thousands and thousands of hours without variation.  Also, the machines where very easy to maintain.  My senior and I where given the job to replace the tones sent to P.A.B.X.s with a tome generator inside each P.A.B.X.

replies.author: @velcroman11
replies.text: At 3:41 now the sparks splashing across the contacts.   The spark is from the “back EMP” produced by the collapsing magnetic field of the relay.  That spark will eventually destroy the contacts.  This is resolved buy placing a small value capacitor across the contacts.  Sometimes a low value resistor is connected in series with the capacitor to slow down the coil discharge and reduce current flow.
